issue_title: Make adding and removing domains more intuitive and less ambiguous
issue_body:
I propose two changes that would make adding and removing domains to/from existing certificates more intuitive and less confusing (and a bit more "automated").

**Change 1**
_Preamble:_ 
I have been using certbot for some time (FreeBSD 10.0.*, certbot 0.10.2, 0.11.1), while reading the documentation and issues and talking to people on #certbot OTC irc channel. 
I was pleasantly surprised (very much!) how easy it was to create and install certs using `--webroot` option with apache 2.4 on FreeBSD. (I had only 8 `--dry-run`'s, of which 3 were to debug --post-hook, and 3-4 were to fix the issues with the correct root directories for a dozen or so domains of which a few are not used for web functions, but for smtp, imap, pop,  - so, they didn't have the corresponding vhosts in apache).

_Issue background_
There were a few options that I've since stumbled upon, and judging from the previous issues here, I am not the only one. The most representative examples include: #2071 and #3664 .
There is a rather confusing `--expand` ( #3664 ). There is a never-implemented `--shrink` (--compress).
Recently, with the new `--lineage` ( #3587 )  option, those two options are obsolete in a way.
I understand that `--expand` is kept, since it's already there, but I suspect it would not have been implemented today, after `--lineage`.

_Existing confusions_
(a) As discussed in #3664 , it was not clear that one has to list **all** the existing domains.
Regardless of the documentation, it would be great to just **add** new domains to the existing set without needing to repeat all of them on the command line.
Note: explicitly listing all of the domains is error prone and makes very little sense if you want to "expand" (i.e. **add**) a domain(s). What you would do is: `certbot certificates` , and then copy and paste it to the new command. That's what programs can do much better than humans.
(b) If you want to remove one domain (SANS) from the existing set, you do almost the same, but just omit the one you want to remove and add `--lineage` option. That has the same drawbacks.

_Proposed change **1**_
  New options:
**`--remove-domain`** 
**`--add-domain`**

With them, a request for the modification of the existing certificate (replacement with a sub- or super-set of the existing domains) would look like this:

**`webroot renew --add-domain new1.domain.com --add-domain new2.domain.com --remove-domain old1.domain.com --lineage mydomain.com`**

The proposed functionality is probably obvious, but just in case: 
Certbot would:
1. Open the existing certificate named mydomain.com (through an error if it doesn't exist)
2. Read all the existing domain names
3., Analyze if _all_ the removal requests can be fulfilled. (otherwise through an error)
4. Add the new domains to the list
5. Request the new certificates and place them into the same lineage name as it is done now with `--lineage`

Side Effect: 
With that the option `--expand` would be deprecated (and eventually dropped, I'd think).


**Change 2**
_Issue background / Existing confusions_
There are some confusions around **renew** -al vs. obtaining **new** certificates with the same **`--lineage`**
Just one example: Do you need `renew` with --lineage?  (or with --expand?)  
(Also, while less relevant here, it shows the degree of confusion:
Some new users think that "renew" does not replace the certificate, just somehow automagically extend the validity of the existing certificate.  )

_Proposed change **2**_
So, I propose the following new command _unambiguously_ reflecting the changes:
 **`modify`**  

With this, the options proposed in **1.** above, would look:

**`webroot modify --add-domain new1.domain.com --add-domain new2.domain.com --remove-domain old1.domain.com --lineage mydomain.com`**


PS. I actually have a different proposal with respect to CLI changes (more drastic), but it is independent of this. So, to avoid any confusion I will be submitting that other one as a separate issue a bit later. 
They overlap (and do not contradict to each other) with respect to **modify**, so, I might reference this issue in the other one.

issue_title: Use ECDSA account keys by default
issue_body: Right now I believe Certbot defaults to RSA account keys. We can reduce bandwidth usage significantly by switching to ECDSA keys, which are also considered stronger. This will also make logger request bodies smaller.

issue_title: Should return non-0 if all certs too recent for renewal
issue_body:
`renew` should return a non zero exit code if all certificates are too recent for renewal. This would allow a script to execute commands only if a successful renewal took place.

The `--renew-hook` option is unsuitable, because it's executed for *each* certificate renewed. But if there are many certificates to be renewed, and the desired action is to restart the web and mail servers, many useless restarts would ensue; thus causing disruptions in ongoing connections and unnecessary CPU and disk load.

Returning a non-zero status is *clearly* the right thing to do. Consider return codes such as EAGAIN (11) or EX_TEMPFAIL (75).

issue_title: Table name expansion uses incorrect escape character for Standard BigQuery SQL
issue_body: For Standard SQL, backticks \` should be used to escape the table name, but we use square brackets [, which are used for legacy BigQuery SQL. When calling a table's `._repr_sql_` method, we get `[project:dataset.tablename]`, which will be used for both legacy and standard SQL.

issue_title: Precision relative to viewbox or max demical point
issue_body:
Ok I re-thought the problem with precision. @Ede123 I think your right about the zero being arbitrary related to #136. This is not an attempt to waste your time and sound like I'm repeat myself.  Not sure if the scour project is interested in going in this direction. But thought I'd share anyway.

I think the reason that rounding near zero worked well for me was not that 0 is special. Its that the precision operation doesn't account for the dimensions of the svg image viewbox. 

If I have a viewbox 100px width 100px height, normally I'm not too concerned with precision like 0.00001. Actually it seems the smallest number i work with is intervals of 0.01.  So 100/0.01 =  10000 units. Which looks like 6 significant digits.

Similarly if i have a viewbox 30px by 30px, using the same 10000 units, my smallest signficant interval would be 0.003. 

So how does a number like 0.00005 slip by scour, but not 1.00005?

The suggestion is to implement some limiting option like --max-decimal-point, or --precision-relative-to-viewbox, or even implicity limit the max to 10,000/100,000 units  the max width/height.

Check instructions and update accordingly.

    dropouti=dropouts[0], wdrop=dropouts[2], dropoute=dropouts[3], dropouth=dropouts[4])
TypeError: __init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'nhid'

The proc_df method seems to drop all ignored fields (ignore_flds) from the provided dataframe (df) right before it gets copied.
If a subset is used this behaviour doesn't happen due to the copy operation within the get_sample method.

First lines of the proc_df method:
```
if not ignore_flds: ignore_flds=[]
if subset: df = get_sample(df,subset)
ignored_flds = df.loc[:, ignore_flds]
df.drop(ignore_flds, axis=1, inplace=True)
df = df.copy()
```

Something like: 
```
if not ignore_flds: ignore_flds=[]
if subset:  
    df = get_sample(df,subset)
else:
    df = df.copy()
ignored_flds = df.loc[:, ignore_flds]
df.drop(ignore_flds, axis=1, inplace=True)
```
might solve this issue.

Within AWS CloudWatch Log Groups, old Log Streams linger until they are pruned through an external process.  While I have not observed this to create any logging failures, I have some Log Groups over four thousand (4,000) empty Log Streams. The command `pulumi logs` appears to run much more slowly on the stacks with excessive log streams, and I hypothesize there to be a correlation.

Strangely, some of my current Log Streams report zero storedBytes, while AWS reports bytes stored on older streams that are outside the retention period. A bug in AWS would not shock me at this point. To at least validate the impact, I'm pruning some of the log groups in our test stack.

issue_title: Actors don't seem to recover when using ray.wait()
issue_body:
The following script can be used to reproduce the issue:

1) Set up a ray cluster with multiple nodes
2) Run the script (with the cluster redis address). The script will submit simple actor tasks at a rate of 100 tasks/s. It then prints out the cumulative number of tasks submitted, completed, pending. It also prints out tasks from which `ray.wait()` returns multiple times (duplicated? -- not sure what's going on there). 
3) When the throughput stabilizes, kill a node manually via e.g. `ray.stop()`.
4) Observe that some tasks remain in `pending` state forever. I've also seen error messages printed out on the driver stdout as well.

I've also tried this with non-actor tasks and saw some tasks that hung, though not as many.

```
import time
import numpy as np
import ray
import random
import threading


@ray.remote(checkpoint_interval=10)
class Counter(object):
    def __init__(self, y):
        self.x = y

    def inc(self, y):
        time.sleep(0.0)
        self.x += y
        return self.x


def setup_actors(num_actors):
    counters = [Counter.remote(1) for _ in range(num_actors)]
    return counters


if __name__ == '__main__':
    address_info = ray.init(redis_address="172.30.1.119:6379")
    pending = []
    counters = setup_actors(200)
    tasks_per_second = 100
    tasks_submitted = 0
    tasks_completed = 0
    duplicated = 0
    start = time.time()
    last_print = start
    i = 0
    seen = set()
    while True:
        tasks_to_launch = (
            (time.time() - start) * tasks_per_second - tasks_submitted)
        while tasks_to_launch > 0:
            pending.append(random.choice(counters).inc.remote(1))
            tasks_submitted += 1
            tasks_to_launch -= 1
        if pending:
            ready, pending = ray.wait(
                pending, num_returns=min(1, len(pending)), timeout=10)
        else:
            time.sleep(.01)
        num_ready = len(ready)
        ready = set(ready).difference(seen)
        duplicated += num_ready - len(ready)
        tasks_completed += len(ready)
        seen = seen.union(ready)
        if time.time() - last_print > 1:
            print(
                "time", i,
                "submitted", tasks_submitted,
                "completed", tasks_completed,
                "running", len(pending),
                "duplicated", duplicated)
            last_print = time.time()
            i += 1
```

cc @stephanie-wang

issue_title: Should we provide functionality for running a function on all workers (or all nodes)?
issue_body:
Should we expose the ability to run a given function on all workers? This can be useful for a bunch of reasons (for example, properly setting the Python path on each worker).

Should we also expose the ability to run a given function once on each node? This can be useful for setting things up (e.g., installing some stuff, downloading some files, copying application files).

issue_title: PPO Rollout + Advantage Estimation should include last state
issue_body:
We drop the last reward at the end of the rollout (when we get the terminal flag). This _should_ be included, and the value of the terminal state should also be estimated. Will fix when we include truncated rollouts into PPO.

Code found here: 
https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/c26c7553bcb0c181e196a0b18dc647791473fc16/python/ray/rllib/ppo/rollout.py#L88

* Package version: 1.7.2
* Django version: 2.0.4
* Python version: 3.6
* Template pack: Bootstrap 4

### Description:

`.col-form-label` seems to get added all the time to labels, even though according to the Bootstrap docs, it's only for horizontal forms. It's not a huge issue, just an unnecessary class in the markup. Could become a problem when one puts styles on it, that you don't want in vertical forms.

There is some pressure to release the fourth version of Bootstrap [1].

Are there plans to update crispy forms template to match?
Is there some current effort ongoing?


[1]http://blog.getbootstrap.com/2016/09/05/bootstrap-4-alpha-4/

Hello Team Django-HelpDesk
First of all , Thank you for publishing this work and making it available, open-source and free of charge.
I started using your code and i'm facing many -many- troubles, i don't know where to start actually.
What i aim by this comment is to help next releases designs decisions and implementation.

App Design:
* The architectural design have many assumptions built-in , like the actual use of local mail server, this becomes clear as queue mail settings does not differentiate between incoming and outgoing settings, 
And, in lib.py line 125 ,i believe, the exception is not casted into `e` so the actual use of this part of code would raise global name e not defined which points out that this part of code was never really executed before.
* It's also very hard to substitute small parts of the application without seriously hacking it. 
You can use options like dotted path for critical functions .. something like  `SEND_TEMPLATED_EMAIL_FUNCTION = 'myapp.handlers.mycustomfunction' `
So i can tweak this critical procedure if i want without overriding forms methods that calls inside the send function. Also you can use swappable models for model tweaking.

Template design:
And i mean the blocks.. It's very hard to simply edit, or integrate the html into the exiting  theme with blocks name always having `helpdesk_` as a prefix in them instead of the generic `extra_head`, `content` etc.

While of course this app is serving you and your use-case at its best, but it's not yet designed to be easily configured for other different uses. And the fact that you're the highest helpdesk/ticketing rated package on djangopackage should (i believe) direct development more towards making it easier to configure, not hack.

Thank you for your time reading this, and for publishing your work -of course- and again.

Caches for prior knowledge about subClassOf relationships for any nodes like diseases in a hierarchy, protein families, components of protein complexes

- [ ] Data model in `sqlalchemy` that connects data in definitions cache with relationships like `subClassOf`, `partOf`, `memberOf`, etc.

```python
SUBCLASS_TABLE_NAME = 'pybel_cache_subclass'

class Subclass(Base):
    """This table represents the many-to-many subclass relationships between names or annotations"""
    __tablename__ = SUBCLASS_TABLE_NAME

    parent = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('{}.id'.format(CONTEXT_TABLE_NAME)), primary_key=True)
    child = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('{}.id'.format(CONTEXT_TABLE_NAME)), primary_key=True)
```

- [ ] Addition of function `add_heirarchy(list_of_pairs)` to `DefinitionCacheManager` that takes a list of pairs `(parent, child)` and inserts the appropriate edges to this table
- [ ] Function polymorphism to also accept a `nx.DiGraph` whose edges are pairs of `(parent, child)`

issue_title: Catching important warnings in test harness
issue_body:
It would be nice if we could make the tests error out on:

* Warning: coroutine '...' was never awaited
* ResourceWarning

What these have in common is that they're both issued by the garbage collector, so the normal way of converting them into errors doesn't work; the only way to detect these is to monkeypatch the `warnings` module, perhaps by adding an `always` filter for the relevant warnings and then installing a custom `warnings.showwarning` hook that records the information somewhere that the test harness can find it.

Some care might be needed to avoid colliding with pytest's warning functionality: https://docs.pytest.org/en/features/warnings.html

Dealing with stdout is a bit awkward because the user is responsible for making sure various apps don’t clobber eachother’s stdout, which adds a lot of boilerplate:
```
for i in range(10):
    stdout = os.path.abspath('std_{}.out'.format(str(i)))

    future = foobar(stdout=stdout)
    future.result()

    with open(stdout, 'r') as f:
        print(f.read())
```

I think 99.9999999% of the time the user doesn't care where the stdout is and doesn't want to be bothered with naming it themselves. I support assigning the stdout a unique name ourselves (with useful info like the task id, and maybe the function name-- for example `foobar_task_1.stdout`), then providing a convenience function `future.stdout()` which would return the path to it:
```
for i in range(10):
    future = foobar()
    future.result()

    with open(future.stdout(), 'r') as f:
        print(f.read())
```

issue_title: Changing the implementation MarkdownX of options in `settings.py`
issue_body:
# New Proposal

This is a new proposal that is put forward for consultation. 

Please feel free to express your opinion, suggest alternative approaches, disagree with or support the ideas expressed throughout this proposal.

---
## Background
Thanks to all the users and contributors across the world, **MarkdownX** has matured considerably and has therefore become quite flexible.

This level of flexibility means that there are a lot of options that may be set or altered in `settings.py`. The number of options available in **MarkdownX** currently stand at 12, all of which are [thoroughly documented](http://neutronx.github.io/django-markdownx/customization.html#quick-reference).

## Problem
As we go on, however, this number will likely increase to accommodate different features, needs, and requests (see #86 for an example). This will inadvertently amount to:
- an overcrowded,  hard to read and follow settings file,
- maintenance difficulties in larger projects,
- slight inconsistency with the methodology employed by Django to address settings with lengthy parameters (e.g. database or middleware settings).

Additionally, to prevent possible conflicts whilst remaining sufficiently explicit, we have had to use quite lengthy variable names. This, especially written in block capitals, is not easy to follow. It is also prone to spelling mistakes. 

## Possible Solution
One way to address this is to set all settings relevant to **MardownX** inside a dictionary. For instance:

In `settings.py`, we set **MardownX** options as follows:
```python
MARKDOWNX = {
    'image': {
        'permitted_extensions': ('jpeg', 'png', 'svg'),
        'max_size': {
            'size': (500, 500),  # px
            'quality': 90  # %
        },
    },

    'editor': {
        'resizable': True,
    },

    'markdown': {
        'interpreter': 'markdownx.utils.markdownify',
        'extensions': ['markdown.extensions.extra'],
    },
    
    'server_call_latency': 500,  # ms
}
```
instead of:
```python
MARKDOWNX_UPLOAD_CONTENT_TYPES = ['image/jpeg', 'image/png', 'image/svg+xml']
MARKDOWNX_IMAGE_MAX_SIZE = {
    'size': (500, 500),  # px
    'quality': 90  # %
}
MARKDOWNX_EDITOR_RESIZABLE = True
MARKDOWNX_SERVER_CALL_LATENCY = 500  # ms
MARKDOWNX_MARKDOWNIFY_FUNCTION = 'markdownx.utils.markdownify'
MARKDOWNX_MARKDOWN_EXTENSIONS = [
    'markdown.extensions.extra'
]
```

Although lengthier, this hierarchical view is more consistent, more extensible, and more contained compared to the existing method. 

## Backwards Compatibility 

If implemented, we shall of course maintain backwards compatibility (with a deprecation warning) until the next major release (version 3). 

## Added Benefit

Implementation of this approach will considerably simplify the processing of setting variables in the [default `settings.py` file](https://github.com/neutronX/django-markdownx/blob/master/markdownx/settings.py) as dictionaries can be merged optimally and easily.

  File "/Users/mrg/git/gitlabcp/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gitlab/mixins.py", line 183, in _check_missing_update_attrs
    raise AttributeError("Missing attributes: %s" % ", ".join(missing))
AttributeError: Missing attributes: file_path, branch, content, commit_message
```

I'm invoking it with the same parameters I used for `create`:

```
           response = p.files.update({
                    'file_path': escaped_path,
                    'branch': args.branch,
                    'content': file_content,
                    'encoding': 'base64',
                    'commit_message': args.commit_message
                    })
```

I see that `create` and `get` are explicitly defined on ProjectFileManager, whereas `update` falls back to the mixin.

If I use the following method instead (hacked version of `create` with `put` instead of `post`), it seems to work:

```
def update(self, data, **kwargs):
    """Updates an existing object
    Args:
        data (dict): parameters to send to the server to create the
                        resource
        **kwargs: Extra options to send to the Gitlab server (e.g. sudo)
    Returns:
        RESTObject: a new instance of the managed object class built with
            the data sent by the server
    Raises:
        GitlabAuthenticationError: If authentication is not correct
        GitlabCreateError: If the server cannot perform the request
    """

    self._check_missing_create_attrs(data)
    file_path = data.pop('file_path')
    path = '%s/%s' % (self.path, file_path)
    server_data = self.gitlab.http_put(path, post_data=data, **kwargs)
    return self._obj_cls(self, server_data)
```

> `group` : **int**
> number of groups input channels and output channels are divided into, default is 1.
> ...
> `W` : `T`
> The weight tensor that will be used in the convolutions; has size (M x C x kH x kW), where C is the number of channels, and kH and kW are the height and width of the kernel, and M is the number of feature maps. For more than 2 dimensions, the kernel shape will be (M x C x k1 x k2 x ... x kn), where is the dimension of the kernel

I think the specification should make it explicit that the filter is of size `(M, C/group, kH, kW)`, as that seems to be the convention (no tests so going by onnx-caffe2/caffe2).

Aside from being clearer, TensorFlow seems to store filters for `Conv2D` in `[kH, kW, C, M]` order, but `DepthwiseConv2dNative` in `[kH, kW, C, 1]`, implying that `M=1`, when in ONNX it would be `C=1`. (`C` being input channels, `M` being output channels)
When will TensorFlow be supported?

issue_title: sr_subscibe download compatibility with PDS 0 permission delivery model.
issue_body:
When delivering a file need a protocol to tell the receiver not to touch it until delivery is complete.
inflight does this for a number of methods. PDS-style method with 0 permissions is not yet supported, and none of the inflight methods are supported by subscribe right now. 

This is an import of bug #63 from sourceforge.net.  from 2017/04.  Since then inflight options have been expanded, better [documented](https://github.com/MetPX/sarracenia/blob/master/doc/sr_subscribe.1.rst#delivery-completion
) and made to work with all components in the same way.
but 0 permission hand-off remains an issue.  

This can't be done with SFTP, protocol does not allow us to set permission bits on file creation on the far end, so it could only be done with an sr_subscribe downloader.  If you have sr_subscribe, then just replace PDS with Sundew and use .tmp...

Eric F. really wants this... not sure why...

Comment 4 by eik...@gmail.com



Comment 5 by eik...@gmail.com
These issues involve bits that have been moved out of rdflib proper for now. We will re-open them 
or move them to rdfextas as appropriate.

Only the basic methods of the socket need to be Cython:

* init
* close
* send
* recv
* getsockopt
* setsockopt

All the derivative and sugar methods (multipart, json, sockopts as attrs, etc.) do not need to be Cython, and do not benefit from being compiled.  I would like to split the Socket file into two: a minimal base class in Cython, and the extended methods on a subclass in Python.

This would reduce compile time, and significantly reduce the need to recompile when working on new features, as the core methods haven't been touched in quite some time.
